behaviors.description: An image containing explicit content is flagged and held for review, not published.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on what the real vision model detects. A mock that always returns 'explicit_content=true' would hide the real failure: the vision model doesn't actually detect explicit content, but the system publishes it anyway. The system's ability to flag explicit images is entirely dependent on the real model's classification capability.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a real image with explicit content through the complete moderation pipeline using the actual vision model API, verify the model classifies it as explicit, and assert the system holds the post for human review rather than publishing it.

behaviors.description: A text post containing hate speech is flagged regardless of obfuscation techniques (e.g., letter substitution, unicode lookalikes).
behaviors.justification: The system's ability to detect obfuscated hate speech is a model capability, not a system logic capability. A mock returning 'hate_speech=true' would hide the real failure: the real text model can't actually understand obfuscation and misses the hate speech. Testing with mocks validates that the system publishes the model's decision, not that the model handles obfuscation correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Submit text posts with obfuscated hate speech (letter substitution like 'h4te', unicode lookalikes like 'н' for 'n', character jumbling) to the real text model through the pipeline, verify the model detects the semantic hate speech despite obfuscation, and confirm posts are held for review.

behaviors.description: A post that passes both content checks is published rather than held or silently dropped.
behaviors.justification: The behavior assumes the content checks have already determined the post is clean. The failure to catch is: post marked clean by both models, but system doesn't publish it (routing logic error). We can mock both models to deterministically return 'clean' and test the publish decision logic. This does not test whether the real models correctly identify clean content—that's covered by behaviors 1-2—but rather that the system routes correctly when both checks pass.
behaviors.test_description: Mock both the vision and text models to return 'clean' classifications, submit a post through the moderation pipeline, and verify the post is published immediately rather than queued for review or silently dropped.

behaviors.description: The full moderation pipeline—image check, text check, publish decision—completes within 5 seconds of submission.
behaviors.justification: The 5-second SLA is end-to-end latency including real AI model API calls. A mock that returns instantly would hide the real failure: the pipeline can't meet the SLA due to model API latency. Testing with mocks only validates that application logic is fast, not that the full system (application + real models) meets the SLA. The test must measure realistic model latency.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a post through the complete pipeline using the real vision and text model APIs (or their staging equivalents), measure the total latency from submission to publish/hold decision, and verify the entire pipeline completes in ≤5 seconds. Run multiple times to account for API variability.

behaviors.description: When the AI vision model is unavailable, image posts are queued for retry rather than auto-approved.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is: vision model throws an error, but the code doesn't queue the post—instead it auto-approves or drops it. We can catch this by mocking the vision model to raise a connection/timeout exception (Integration level). We don't need the real vision model to actually be down; a mock exception is sufficient to test the exception handling and retry queueing logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the vision model to raise a ConnectionError or TimeoutError when called, submit an image post to the pipeline, and verify the post is added to a retry queue rather than published or discarded.

behaviors.description: The moderation decision for a borderline image is consistent when the same image is submitted multiple times within a short window.
behaviors.justification: A mock returning deterministically 'borderline_flagged' would always pass, hiding the real failure: the real vision model sometimes classifies the borderline image one way and sometimes another across multiple submissions (model non-determinism). To catch this, we must test with the real model and observe whether identical image submissions within a time window receive identical classifications. This is a statistical property of the real model, not application logic.
behaviors.test_description: Select a borderline image (manually verified to be near the classification boundary). Submit it to the real vision model through the pipeline three times within 60 seconds. Verify all three submissions receive the same moderation decision (either all flagged or all approved). Repeat this test 10+ times with different borderline images to detect model non-determinism.
